tree.heading: 1. Introduction
tree.content_preview: Haemodialysis continues to expand in the UK with over 25 000 patients now being treated, representing a 10% increase since publication of the previous Renal Association guideline for haemodialysis. In

tree.heading: 1. Dialysis dose in thrice weekly dialysis schedules
tree.content_preview: We recommend eKt/V as the most clinically valid small-solute measure of dialysis dose, and recommend monitoring of dialysis dose on a monthly basis for the majority of centre-based dialysis patients.

children.heading: Guideline 2.1 Augmented schedules
children.content_preview:
We suggest offering an augmented schedule to patients who are unable to achieve adequacy targets or fluid control on a standard thrice weekly schedule. [2B]

children.heading: Guideline 2.2 Incremental schedules
children.content_preview:
We suggest that lower haemodialysis dose targets may be optimal in patients with significant residual renal function. [2D]

We recommend that residual renal function should be quantified intermittentl

children.heading: Guideline 2.3 Conservative schedules
children.content_preview: We suggest that lower haemodialysis dose targets may be optimal when quality of life is the primary goal of treatment, rather than longevity. [2D]

children.heading: Guideline 2.4 Paediatric schedules
children.content_preview: In children and adolescents we recommend an approach to the assessment of dialysis adequacy which goes beyond biochemical targets, incorporating clinical goals such as growth, bone health, cardiac fun

tree.content_preview:
We recommend that patients with minimal residual function should be treated with high-flux dialysers. [1B]

We recommend assessment of fluid status when prompted by clinical circumstances, and on a quarterly basis for stable patients. [1C]

children.content_preview:
We recommend an optimal pre-dialysis serum potassium in the range 4.0-6.0mmol/L, remembering to consider measurement errors (e.g. due to haemolysis) when interpreting levels. [1B]

children.heading: Guideline 5.2 Selection of dialysate buffer
children.content_preview:
We recommend an optimal pre-dialysis serum bicarbonate in the range 18.0-26.0mmo/L, remembering to consider measurement errors (e.g. due to exposure to air) when interpreting levels. [1C]

children.heading: Guideline 5.3 Supplementation of dialysate with phosphate
children.content_preview: We suggest considering supplementation of the dialysate with phosphate in patients on augmented dialysis schedules. [2D]

children.heading: Guideline 5.4 Paediatric dialysate considerations
children.content_preview:
We recommend individualisation of dialysate electrolyte concentrations, including potassium, buffer and calcium. [1C]

tree.heading: 6. Anticoagulation
tree.content_preview:
We recommend that patients without increased bleeding risk should be given unfractionated or lowmolecular-weight heparin during dialysis to reduce clotting of the extracorporeal system. [1A]

We recom

children.heading: Guideline 7.1 Routine blood loss
children.content_preview: We suggest that during washback, dialysis lines and dialyser are observed to ensure residual blood loss is kept to a minimum. [2C]

children.heading: Guideline 7.2 Disconnection haemorrhage
children.content_preview: We recommend maintaining awareness of the risk of disconnection, the limitations of pressure alarms, and importance of direct observation, through a program of education, including patients and carers

children.heading: Guideline 7.3 Immune reactions during dialysis
children.content_preview: We recommend that dialysis staff should be aware of the features and management of dialysis reactions, and should have access to a range of dialyser types. [1C]

children.heading: Guideline 8.1 Home haemodialysis
children.content_preview:
We recommend that home haemodialysis should be available in all units as part of a comprehensive renal replacement therapy programme. [1A]

We suggest training patients and/or care partners to achieve

children.heading: Guideline 8.2 Shared haemodialysis care
children.content_preview: We suggest that all centre-based haemodialysis patients should have opportunity and encouragement to learn aspects of their dialysis treatment, and take an active role in their care. [2D]

children.heading: Guideline 8.3 Intradialytic exercise
children.content_preview:
We recommend that intradialytic exercise should be available in all units, as a treatment for enhancing physical functioning, in patients without contraindications. [1B]

We suggest that intradialytic

children.heading: Guideline 8.4 Dialysis experience for children and adolescents
children.content_preview: We recommend that haemodialysis for children and adolescents should be delivered in a dedicated paediatric dialysis centre or at home, with the involvement of a paediatric multidisciplinary team. [1C]

tree.heading: 3. Summary of audit measures
tree.content_preview:
Audit Measure 1: Amongst thrice-weekly patients on dialysis for more than a year, the median eKt/V, and proportion achieving eKt/V at least 1.2.

tree.heading: 1. Dialysis dose in thrice weekly dialysis schedules
tree.content_preview: We recommend eKt/V as the most clinically valid small-solute measure of dialysis dose, and recommend monitoring of dialysis dose on a monthly basis for the majority of centre-based dialysis patients.

tree.heading: Rationale

tree.heading: Dialysis time
tree.content_preview:
The optimum treatment duration for thrice weekly haemodialysis is slightly less clear, since it is difficult to separate the effect of treatment time from dialysis dose [16].

The evolution of dialysi

tree.heading: Summary
tree.content_preview: Optimal outcomes in patients on thrice weekly dialysis are achieved with sessions of at least 4 hours, providing eKt/V at least 1.2. Regular monitoring is strongly recommended, and this occurs monthly

children.content_preview:
We recommend an optimal pre-dialysis serum bicarbonate in the range 18.0-26.0mmo/L, remembering to consider measurement errors (e.g. due to exposure to air) when interpreting levels. [1C]

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: We suggest a combined approach to abnormal pre-dialysis serum bicarbonate, which may include increasing dialysis dose, oral bicarbonate, nutritional support, or individualising dialysate buffer. [2D]

tree.heading: Rationale

children.content_preview:

tree.content_preview:
Adult guidelines for dialysate composition (sections 5.1 - 5.3) are generally applicable to children, though there are a number of additional considerations.

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Platelet activation in the extracorporeal circuit accelerates thrombin generation via the intrinsic coagulation pathway, so that anticoagulation is usually required to prevent thrombosis. Unfractionat

children.heading: Guideline 7.1 Routine blood loss
children.content_preview: We suggest that during washback, dialysis lines and dialyser are observed to ensure residual blood loss is kept to a minimum. [2C]

tree.heading: Rationale

tree.heading: Rationale

children.heading: Guideline 7.3 Immune reactions during dialysis
children.content_preview: We recommend that dialysis staff should be aware of the features and management of dialysis reactions, and should have access to a range of dialyser types. [1C]

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview:
From the early 1980s reports appeared describing abrupt clinical reactions occurring soon after the onset of dialysis [1]. These have traditionally been classified into two types.

children.content_preview: We recommend that haemodialysis for children and adolescents should be delivered in a dedicated paediatric dialysis centre or at home, with the involvement of a paediatric multidisciplinary team. [1C]

tree.content_preview: This guideline is written primarily for doctors and nurses working in dialysis units and related areas of medicine in the UK, and is an update of a previous version written in 2009. It aims to provide
